text: Goal maintenance — holding a task goal active in the face of distraction — is perhaps the most essential function the prefrontal cortex performs during cognitive work. Jonathan Cohen at Princeton demonstrated that the PFC maintains 'goal representations' that act as a filter on incoming information: if a stimulus is relevant to the current goal, it receives amplified processing; if it is not, it is attenuated. The irony of modern notification design is that it engineers stimuli to appear goal-relevant — a message badge mimics the social urgency that would historically have warranted immediate attention — thereby hijacking the very filtering mechanism meant to protect focus. The PFC cannot easily distinguish a genuine emergency from a cleverly designed ping.
question: According to Jonathan Cohen's research, how does the PFC determine which incoming stimuli receive amplified processing?

options.text: It prioritizes stimuli that arrive with high sensory salience or sudden movement
It amplifies stimuli relevant to the current goal and attenuates those that are not
It relies on the amygdala to flag emotionally significant incoming information
It processes all stimuli equally before deciding which to suppress
